heroOverview: Schwannomatosis is a rare genetic tumour-predisposition syndrome characterised by the development of multiple schwannomas — benign tumours of the Schwann cells that encase peripheral nerves — throughout the body, combined with a distinctive chronic pain syndrome that is often debilitating and out of proportion to tumour size. Unlike neurofibromatosis type 2, schwannomatosis does not cause bilateral vestibular schwannomas and generally spares hearing. It affects approximately 1 in 40,000–70,000 individuals and is the third most common neurofibromatosis-family syndrome. Germline mutations in SMARCB1 or LZTR1 are identified in approximately 85% of familial cases and 30–40% of apparently sporadic cases; a proportion of remaining cases have no currently identified molecular cause. Chronic pain, rather than tumour number, is the dominant driver of morbidity and quality-of-life impairment.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 40,000–70,000 individuals; about one-third of cases are familial
keyStats.demographics: Diagnosis typically in the third to fifth decade; no sex predominance; can present at any age
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age at symptom onset approximately 30–40 years; diagnosis often delayed by a decade

definition: Schwannomatosis (ICD-10: Q85.03) is an autosomal-dominant genetic syndrome defined by the presence of two or more non-intradermal schwannomas with at least one pathologically confirmed, or one non-vestibular schwannoma plus a first-degree relative with schwannomatosis, in the absence of bilateral vestibular (acoustic) schwannomas. Schwannomas are benign tumours originating from Schwann cells — the myelin-forming cells of the peripheral nervous system. In schwannomatosis, tumours can arise on any peripheral, cranial, or spinal nerve and tend to grow slowly, but their relationship to adjacent neural structures causes neuropathic pain, weakness, or sensory disturbance. The molecular basis involves a two-hit tumour-suppressor mechanism at the SMARCB1 locus (chromosome 22q11.23) or the LZTR1 locus (chromosome 22q11.21), genes that encode subunits of the SWI/SNF chromatin-remodelling complex. Germline SMARCB1 variants are associated with spinal and peripheral nerve schwannomas and with a small risk of rhabdoid tumours in young children. LZTR1 variants are more common in familial cases and are associated with a higher number of schwannomas. A third gene, NF2, is mutated within individual tumours in a mosaic pattern but germline NF2 mutations are absent in classic schwannomatosis — this genomic instability at 22q is characteristic. Schwannomatosis falls under neuroscience, clinical genetics, and pain medicine. Sporadic cases — with no family history and no identified germline variant — account for the majority of diagnoses. Meningiomas occur in approximately 5–8% of SMARCB1 schwannomatosis cases.

typesClassification.description: Caused by germline SMARCB1 mutations; accounts for approximately 40–50% of familial cases. Associated with spinal and peripheral schwannomas; carries a small risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours in the proband's children.

Caused by germline LZTR1 mutations; accounts for approximately 30% of familial cases. May present with more numerous tumours and a higher frequency of diagnostic criteria being met in first-degree relatives.

Segmental or somatic mosaicism for NF2 mutations can produce a phenotype resembling schwannomatosis without bilateral vestibular schwannomas; requires careful molecular analysis to distinguish from classic germline NF2 disease.
No identifiable germline mutation or family history; the most common presentation. These patients may harbour low-level somatic mosaicism not detectable with standard testing.
Tumours confined to one body region (e.g., a single limb or spinal region); thought to represent somatic mosaicism for a schwannomatosis mutation arising early in development.

primarySymptoms: Chronic, often severe pain — typically described as burning, aching, or shooting — in the region of a schwannoma; this is the dominant symptom and is frequently refractory to standard analgesics.
A palpable, slowly growing subcutaneous or deep tissue lump along the course of a peripheral nerve, usually firm and slightly mobile.
Focal neurological signs including numbness, tingling, or weakness in the distribution of the affected nerve when tumour compression is significant.
Allodynia — pain from stimuli that would not normally be painful, such as light touch — in areas served by affected nerves.
Spinal pain radiating into the limbs when schwannomas arise from spinal nerve roots, mimicking radiculopathy.
Positional pain exacerbated by specific movements or postures that increase pressure on a tumour-bearing nerve.
Headache or cranial nerve symptoms when schwannomas involve non-vestibular cranial nerves such as the trigeminal or facial nerve.
Progressive numbness or sensory loss in a limb, sometimes fluctuating with changes in tumour size.
Muscle weakness in a nerve distribution, though frank muscle wasting is uncommon given the usually benign, slow-growing nature of schwannomas.
Significant sleep disruption and reduced quality of life driven by uncontrolled neuropathic pain, independent of overall tumour burden.
earlyWarningSigns: A new deep aching or burning pain in a limb, trunk, or back that persists for weeks without an obvious mechanical cause.
A palpable soft tissue lump adjacent to or along the path of a known nerve, even if painless.
Asymmetric numbness or tingling in a limb that does not match a standard dermatome pattern and does not resolve.
A first-degree relative diagnosed with schwannomatosis, making surveillance and genetic testing urgent.
emergencySigns: Rapidly progressive limb weakness or paralysis from spinal cord compression by a schwannoma — requires emergency MRI and surgical consultation.
Loss of bladder or bowel control from spinal cord or cauda equina compression, a surgical emergency.
Sudden severe headache with neck stiffness, suggesting a schwannoma has caused intracranial pathology requiring urgent imaging.
A rapidly growing or changing tumour with constitutional symptoms such as fever or night sweats, raising concern for malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour transformation — rare but recognised.

causes.cause: Germline SMARCB1 mutation with somatic second hit
causes.description: One copy of SMARCB1 is inactivated in the germline; a somatic second hit in a Schwann cell progenitor inactivates the remaining copy, releasing chromatin remodelling control and allowing unrestricted Schwann cell proliferation — the classic two-hit tumour suppressor mechanism.

causes.cause: Germline LZTR1 mutation with somatic second hit
causes.description: LZTR1 encodes a cullin 3-dependent ubiquitin ligase adapter that regulates RAS-MAPK signalling; biallelic inactivation in a Schwann cell progenitor drives tumour formation in a manner analogous to SMARCB1 loss.

NF2 mutations arising postzygotically in a founder Schwann cell produce a clone susceptible to further hits; this mechanism explains schwannomatosis in patients with no germline variant found and may underlie a significant proportion of apparently sporadic cases.
Approximately 15–20% of schwannomatosis cases meeting clinical criteria have no identifiable variant in SMARCB1, LZTR1, or NF2; new genes and epigenetic mechanisms are actively being studied.

Familial schwannomatosis is inherited as an autosomal-dominant trait, but penetrance is approximately 15–25%, meaning many carriers never develop clinically significant disease; this variable expression often delays recognition of a family pattern.

riskFactors.description: A first-degree relative with confirmed schwannomatosis confers a 15–25% lifetime risk to each child due to autosomal-dominant inheritance with reduced penetrance.
A confirmed pathogenic variant in either gene establishes lifetime susceptibility and indicates the need for regular imaging surveillance.

riskFactors.factor: Previous schwannoma at an unusual site or age
riskFactors.description: A single schwannoma in a young adult (under 40) or at an unexpected site such as a spinal root should prompt evaluation for schwannomatosis syndrome rather than assumption of a sporadic lesion.

riskFactors.factor: SMARCB1 variant with family planning implications
riskFactors.description: Germline SMARCB1 mutations carry a small risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours in the proband's children — a distinct and serious concern that makes genetic counselling in this subgroup especially important.
Neuropathic pain without identified cause in a young to middle-aged adult, especially with any peripheral soft-tissue mass, is an indication for specialist evaluation and whole-body MRI.

affectedDemographics: Adults typically diagnosed in the third to fifth decade; onset of symptoms often in the twenties
Males and females approximately equally affected
First-degree relatives of individuals with confirmed schwannomatosis who require proactive surveillance
Patients with a previous surgically confirmed schwannoma now presenting with a second lesion or unexplained pain
Children of SMARCB1 carriers require paediatric tumour surveillance given the rare risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours
diagnosisOverview: Schwannomatosis is diagnosed on clinical grounds using established criteria: two or more pathologically confirmed non-intradermal schwannomas with at least one diagnosed after age 30, in the absence of bilateral vestibular schwannomas, and with no NF2 germline mutation. The diagnostic evaluation begins with a detailed history of pain character, distribution, and duration — neuropathic pain in schwannomatosis is strikingly disproportionate to tumour burden and often precedes imaging detection. Whole-body MRI is the cornerstone investigation, providing a non-irradiating survey of all schwannoma locations including intraspinal, paraspinal, and peripheral nerves. Spinal MRI is particularly important because spinal schwannomas, which may be entirely asymptomatic on clinical examination, are present in the majority of patients when imaged. Molecular genetic testing for SMARCB1 and LZTR1 germline variants is recommended in all patients meeting clinical criteria, both to confirm the diagnosis and because the specific gene and variant type have management implications (SMARCB1 rhabdoid risk, LZTR1 tumour burden). A negative genetic result does not exclude the diagnosis when clinical criteria are met. Surgical pathology confirming schwannoma histology — with characteristic Antoni A and B patterns and strong S-100 positivity — provides definitive tissue confirmation. In uncertain cases, loss of SMARCB1 immunostaining in tumour tissue supports the diagnosis. Audiology and formal audiometry should confirm normal hearing and the absence of bilateral vestibular schwannomas to exclude NF2.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the number, size, and location of schwannomas throughout the body without radiation; it is the primary imaging tool for establishing tumour burden and for surveillance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in a large scanner for 60–90 minutes; the scan is noisy but painless. Gadolinium contrast enhances schwannoma visibility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects intraspinal and paraspinal schwannomas on nerve roots that cause radicular pain; identifies spinal cord compression requiring surgical intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A separate focused MRI of the spine lasting 30–45 minutes, usually with IV gadolinium contrast injected into the arm.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms schwannoma diagnosis by identifying characteristic biphasic Antoni A and B tissue patterns and strong S-100 protein immunostaining.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A surgeon removes the tumour or takes a sample under local or general anaesthesia; the specimen is sent to pathology for microscopic analysis.

diagnosticTests.test: Germline molecular testing (SMARCB1 and LZTR1 sequencing)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative mutation in approximately 85% of familial and 30–40% of sporadic cases, enabling family cascade testing and risk stratification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is taken; sequencing results typically return in 4–8 weeks from a specialist genetics laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes bilateral hearing loss from vestibular schwannomas, which would indicate NF2 rather than schwannomatosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You wear headphones in a sound booth and press a button when you hear tones; the test takes 20–30 minutes and is entirely non-invasive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Loss of SMARCB1 protein staining in the schwannoma nucleus supports SMARCB1-associated schwannomatosis and is a useful adjunct when germline testing is unavailable or negative.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on tissue already removed at surgery; no additional procedure is required.

treatmentOverview: Schwannomatosis has no medical treatment that slows tumour growth or prevents new schwannomas. Management focuses on two distinct priorities: controlling the chronic neuropathic pain that dominates quality of life, and surgical or interventional management of specific symptomatic tumours. Pain management in schwannomatosis requires a specialist pain medicine team because standard analgesic ladders are frequently inadequate. First-line agents include gabapentinoids (gabapentin, pregabalin) and tricyclic antidepressants for neuropathic pain; opioids are used judiciously for severe pain not controlled by other means, with awareness of dependence risk in a condition that is lifelong. Targeted neurolytic interventions — nerve blocks, radiofrequency ablation — can provide temporary relief for selected nerve distributions. Surgical resection of a schwannoma is indicated when it is causing significant pain, neurological deficit, or functional impairment, and when imaging suggests the tumour is technically removable without unacceptable nerve damage. Complete en-bloc resection is the goal because incomplete removal risks recurrence. Intraoperative nerve monitoring is standard to preserve nerve function during tumour dissection. Radiation, including stereotactic radiosurgery, is generally avoided because schwannomas in this syndrome are numerous and repeat radiation exposure increases secondary malignancy risk. Bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF antibody) has been used off-label in case series and shows modest pain and tumour-volume responses in selected patients, drawing on experience from NF2 vestibular schwannoma treatment. Surveillance imaging with whole-body MRI every one to three years allows early identification of new or growing tumours before they cause irreversible nerve damage.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin or pregabalin
medicalTreatments.description: Calcium channel alpha-2-delta ligands that reduce neuropathic pain by modulating ectopic neural firing; first-line pharmacological treatment for schwannomatosis-related neuropathic pain, titrated gradually to therapeutic effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40–60% of patients with neuropathic pain conditions achieve at least 50% pain reduction with gabapentinoids in randomised trials; response in schwannomatosis is variable.

medicalTreatments.name: Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose tricyclics modulate pain processing through noradrenaline and serotonin reuptake inhibition and sodium channel blockade; used as adjuncts or alternatives to gabapentinoids for neuropathic pain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NNT of approximately 3–4 for 50% pain relief in neuropathic pain conditions; combined with gabapentinoids in refractory cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label intravenous bevacizumab has been used in small case series of schwannomatosis with significant pain burden; some patients show partial tumour volume reduction and pain improvement.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50% of treated patients showed imaging responses in the largest published schwannomatosis series; evidence base is limited to case series, not randomised trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Opioid analgesics
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe refractory neuropathic pain under specialist supervision with structured monitoring for dependence and function; preferably combined with non-opioid agents to limit dose requirements.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provide partial pain relief in many patients but do not address the neuropathic mechanism; benefit must be weighed against dependence and cognitive side effects over a lifelong condition.

medicalTreatments.name: Regional nerve blocks and radiofrequency ablation
medicalTreatments.description: Targeted injection of local anaesthetic or corticosteroid around an affected nerve, or radiofrequency ablation of a pain-generating nerve segment, provides temporary but clinically meaningful relief in selected anatomical targets.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Duration of benefit varies from weeks to months; an estimated 60–70% of patients experience meaningful short-term relief from targeted nerve blocks in observational reports.

surgicalOptions.name: Schwannoma resection
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of a symptomatic schwannoma using intraoperative nerve monitoring to preserve axonal continuity; complete excision reduces recurrence compared with subtotal removal.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70–80% of operations achieve complete resection; neurological deficit occurs in roughly 5–10% of cases depending on tumour location.

surgicalOptions.name: Microsurgical nerve-sparing excision
surgicalOptions.description: For tumours tightly adherent to nerve fascicles, microsurgical technique dissects the tumour from the nerve under magnification, accepting some residual tumour to preserve motor function.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Motor function is preserved in over 90% of cases when experienced microsurgeons use intraoperative monitoring.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured eight-week mindfulness programmes have shown efficacy in chronic pain conditions; some schwannomatosis centres incorporate MBSR as part of multidisciplinary pain management.

alternativeTreatments.name: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)
alternativeTreatments.description: TENS units deliver low-level electrical pulses to the skin to modulate pain perception; used as an adjunct for localised neuropathic pain, with variable but generally modest benefit.

whySeeSpecialist: Schwannomatosis should be managed at a specialist neurofibromatosis or genetic tumour syndrome centre experienced with the condition. Pain management requires a dedicated pain medicine team; neurosurgical planning for spinal and peripheral tumours requires experience with nerve-sparing technique; and genetic counselling is essential given the SMARCB1 rhabdoid tumour risk to offspring. Patients who have exhausted standard pain management benefit from multidisciplinary pain programmes familiar with the neuropathic mechanisms of this specific syndrome.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a neurofibromatosis clinical network centre that has established protocols for schwannomatosis including genetic testing, whole-body MRI surveillance, neurosurgery, and pain medicine. Your genetic team should include a counsellor experienced in communicating the specific SMARCB1 rhabdoid risk to families. A pain medicine specialist with neuro-oncology or neuropathic pain expertise is preferable to a general pain clinic. Ask about the centre's volume of schwannomatosis patients annually and whether they participate in clinical trials for novel therapies.
preventionStrategies: Genetic testing and counselling for first-degree relatives of confirmed cases, to identify carriers and initiate surveillance before symptoms develop.
Regular whole-body MRI surveillance every one to three years for confirmed carriers or clinical cases, to detect growing tumours before they cause irreversible nerve damage.
Preimplantation genetic diagnosis for couples where one partner carries a germline SMARCB1 or LZTR1 variant, avoiding transmission to children.
Paediatric tumour surveillance for children of SMARCB1 carriers, given the rare but serious risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours in the first years of life.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain regular low-impact physical activity — swimming, cycling, yoga — which supports neuroplasticity and helps modulate central sensitisation underlying chronic pain.
Engage in a specialist pain psychology programme; catastrophising and pain-related fear are independent drivers of disability in schwannomatosis beyond pain intensity itself.
Keep a pain and symptom diary to identify patterns, triggers, and treatment responses to share with the care team.
Plan activities to accommodate pain variability — pacing is more effective than avoidance for sustaining function.
Inform surgical and anaesthetic teams of the diagnosis before any procedure, as nerve-adjacent anaesthesia carries specific risks.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory dietary pattern — Mediterranean diet with high fruits, vegetables, oily fish, and whole grains — which may modulate systemic inflammation relevant to neuropathic pain
Adequate protein and micronutrients to support nerve health and general wellbeing
Consistent meal timing and sleep hygiene, which reduce central sensitisation and pain amplification
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which worsens neuropathic pain and interacts adversely with gabapentinoids and tricyclic antidepressants
Highly processed and high-sugar diets that exacerbate systemic inflammation
Caffeine excess in patients with sleep disruption from pain, as it further worsens sleep quality

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise reduces chronic pain through endorphin release and central sensitisation modulation. Low-impact activities such as swimming, cycling, and walking are well tolerated and do not pose nerve injury risk. Contact sports are generally avoided for tumours involving superficial nerves where direct impact could cause neural injury. Exercise should be paced to avoid post-exertional pain flare — short sessions (20–30 minutes) of moderate intensity, several times per week, are typically better tolerated than infrequent intense sessions.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed neuropathic pain medications at consistent times daily to maintain steady therapeutic blood levels.
Apply heat or cold packs to painful regions as an adjunct to pharmacological management.
Use adaptive tools for tasks made difficult by weakness or sensory loss in affected nerve distributions.
Attend annual review at the specialist centre for pain re-assessment and consideration of surgical options for new or growing tumours.
Seek immediate evaluation for any rapidly worsening weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control, or sudden severe neurological change.
prognosis: Schwannomatosis is a lifelong condition but is not associated with reduced life expectancy in most patients. The majority of schwannomas remain benign and grow slowly. The dominant burden is chronic neuropathic pain, which can be severe and refractory, significantly impairing quality of life, employment, and social function in a proportion of patients. Multidisciplinary pain management improves function in the majority of cases, though complete pain relief is rarely achieved. Surgical resection of symptomatic tumours provides durable relief in well-selected cases. Malignant transformation to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is rare in schwannomatosis — distinctly less common than in neurofibromatosis type 1 — and life expectancy approaches the general population in the absence of this complication. Children of SMARCB1 mutation carriers have a small but important risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours, which is managed with paediatric tumour surveillance.
recoveryTimeline: Post-surgical schwannoma resection: most patients experience improvement or resolution of tumour-related symptoms over two to twelve weeks, depending on tumour size and surgical approach. Neuropathic pain improvement after surgery is variable — some patients have immediate relief, others experience no change or worsening. Pain pharmacotherapy is adjusted over weeks to months as response becomes apparent. Whole-body MRI surveillance continues throughout life, typically annually for the first few years and then every two to three years for stable disease.
complications: Chronic refractory neuropathic pain — the most common and debilitating complication; affects up to 95% of patients and drives the greatest reduction in quality of life.
Nerve damage from tumour compression — results in weakness, sensory loss, or dysaesthesia that may be permanent if decompression is delayed.
Post-surgical neurological deficit — approximately 5–10% of schwannoma resections result in some degree of new or worsened sensory or motor deficit.
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST) — rare transformation to malignancy, estimated in under 1% of schwannomatosis patients, but associated with poor prognosis.
Malignant rhabdoid tumour in children of SMARCB1 carriers — a serious but rare complication requiring paediatric surveillance and early detection.
Psychological complications — anxiety, depression, and loss of employment are common sequelae of uncontrolled chronic pain in this condition.

supportResources.name: Children's Tumor Foundation — Schwannomatosis
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient and family resource on schwannomatosis, covering genetics, diagnosis, treatment, and clinical trials.

supportResources.name: NORD — Schwannomatosis
supportResources.description: Rare disease overview including diagnostic criteria, current research, and support organisation links.

supportResources.name: NIH GARD — Schwannomatosis
supportResources.description: NIH patient information resource summarising inheritance, genetics, and management of schwannomatosis.

sources.title: Evans DG et al. Schwannomatosis: a genetic and epidemiological study. Journal of Neurology Neurosurgery and Psychiatry, 2018

confusedWithConditions.name: Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NF2 causes bilateral vestibular schwannomas that damage hearing; schwannomatosis does not cause bilateral vestibular schwannomas and generally spares hearing. Both produce multiple schwannomas, but the gene, inheritance pattern, and clinical profile differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NF1 causes neurofibromas (not schwannomas), café-au-lait spots, Lisch nodules, and a higher malignant transformation rate. NF1 and schwannomatosis have different gene mutations and clinical findings.

confusedWithConditions.name: Meningioma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Meningiomas arise from the meninges, not Schwann cells, and are a brain/spinal tumour rather than a peripheral nerve tumour. They can co-occur in SMARCB1-schwannomatosis but are distinct lesions.

relatedConditions.name: Neurofibromatosis type 2
relatedConditions.relevance: NF2 and schwannomatosis both predispose to schwannomas and share chromosome 22 instability; distinguishing them guides clinical management, particularly hearing surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Neurofibromatosis type 1
relatedConditions.relevance: NF1 is the commonest neurofibromatosis-family syndrome; its clinical features and management differ substantially from schwannomatosis but it is part of the same diagnostic spectrum.

relatedConditions.name: Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma)
relatedConditions.relevance: A unilateral vestibular schwannoma may be the presenting tumour in schwannomatosis; bilateral acoustic neuromas point to NF2 instead.

relatedConditions.name: Meningioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Meningiomas co-occur in approximately 5–8% of SMARCB1-associated schwannomatosis cases and are detected on whole-body MRI surveillance.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple endocrine neoplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Another genetic tumour-predisposition syndrome with autosomal-dominant inheritance; relevant in the differential diagnosis when multiple tumours are found.

faqs.question: What is schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Schwannomatosis is a genetic condition that causes multiple benign tumours called schwannomas to grow on the nerves throughout the body, combined with chronic neuropathic pain. It is distinct from neurofibromatosis and generally does not affect hearing.

faqs.question: What causes schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Most cases are caused by inherited mutations in the SMARCB1 or LZTR1 genes on chromosome 22. These mutations impair tumour suppressor function in Schwann cells. About one-third of cases are familial and two-thirds appear sporadic, though many sporadic cases may carry undetectable somatic mutations.

faqs.question: Is schwannomatosis the same as neurofibromatosis?
faqs.answer: They are related but distinct. Schwannomatosis, NF1, and NF2 all belong to the neurofibromatosis family of conditions but involve different genes, different tumour types, and different clinical features. Schwannomatosis produces multiple schwannomas without the bilateral hearing tumours of NF2 or the skin features of NF1.

faqs.question: Does schwannomatosis cause hearing loss?
faqs.answer: Schwannomatosis does not typically cause hearing loss because it spares the vestibular (hearing) nerves. Bilateral hearing loss from vestibular schwannomas points to NF2, not schwannomatosis. Formal audiometry is part of the diagnostic workup to exclude NF2.

faqs.question: Why is schwannomatosis so painful?
faqs.answer: The pain in schwannomatosis is neuropathic — generated by nerve compression and central sensitisation — and is often disproportionate to tumour size. It can involve entire limbs or the spine and is frequently described as burning, shooting, or aching. Standard analgesics are often insufficient, requiring specialist pain management.

faqs.question: How is schwannomatosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires two or more pathologically confirmed schwannomas without bilateral vestibular schwannomas. Whole-body MRI maps all tumours. Genetic testing for SMARCB1 and LZTR1 confirms the molecular cause in about 85% of familial cases. A specialist neurofibromatosis centre coordinates the workup.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: There is no drug that prevents schwannoma growth. Treatment focuses on pain management using gabapentinoids, tricyclic antidepressants, nerve blocks, and sometimes opioids. Symptomatic tumours are surgically removed using nerve-sparing techniques. Bevacizumab has shown benefit in some patients in case series.

faqs.question: Can schwannomas in schwannomatosis become cancerous?
faqs.answer: Malignant transformation to malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour is rare in schwannomatosis — occurring in under 1% of patients — and less common than in NF1. Any rapidly growing or changing tumour should be promptly evaluated with imaging and, if necessary, biopsy.

faqs.question: Is schwannomatosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes, schwannomatosis is autosomal dominant — one faulty copy of SMARCB1 or LZTR1 is enough to cause susceptibility. However, penetrance is only 15–25%, so many carriers never develop significant disease. About one-third of cases have an affected first-degree relative.

faqs.question: What genes are mutated in schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: The two main genes are SMARCB1 (chromosome 22q11.23) and LZTR1 (chromosome 22q11.21). SMARCB1 variants account for about 40–50% of familial cases; LZTR1 about 30%. Up to 20% of patients have no identified variant with current testing.

faqs.question: What surveillance is recommended for schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Whole-body MRI every one to three years is standard to detect new or growing tumours before they cause irreversible nerve damage. First-degree relatives of confirmed cases should have genetic testing and, if positive, begin surveillance imaging. Children of SMARCB1 carriers need paediatric tumour surveillance.

faqs.question: How does schwannomatosis affect daily life?
faqs.answer: Chronic pain is the dominant impact. Many patients have difficulty sleeping, working, and performing physical tasks. Multidisciplinary pain management, pacing strategies, and psychological support significantly improve daily function. Surgery relieves symptoms in selected cases with well-located accessible tumours.

faqs.question: What is the risk of passing schwannomatosis to children?
faqs.answer: Each child of an affected parent has a 50% chance of inheriting the gene variant. However, penetrance is low — only about 15–25% of carriers develop clinically significant disease. Preimplantation genetic testing is available for couples who wish to avoid transmission.

faqs.question: Can schwannomatosis be cured?
faqs.answer: There is no cure currently available. Individual symptomatic schwannomas can be surgically removed, and pain can be managed with medications and procedures. Research into pathway-targeted therapies, including MEK inhibitors and anti-VEGF agents, is ongoing at specialist centres.

faqs.question: What is the difference between schwannomatosis and acoustic neuroma?
faqs.answer: An acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) is a single tumour on the hearing nerve; it can occur sporadically or in NF2. Schwannomatosis involves multiple schwannomas across various nerves and specifically does not include bilateral vestibular schwannomas. A solitary unilateral acoustic neuroma may be the first manifestation of schwannomatosis.

faqs.question: Do children get schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Schwannomatosis usually presents in adulthood, typically in the third to fifth decade. However, children of SMARCB1 carriers face a rare but serious risk of malignant rhabdoid tumours — a distinct aggressive tumour type separate from schwannomas — requiring specialised paediatric surveillance in the first years of life.

faqs.question: What pain medications work best for schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Gabapentin and pregabalin are first-line for neuropathic pain; tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline are second-line adjuncts. Opioids are used for severe refractory pain under specialist supervision. Nerve blocks and radiofrequency ablation provide targeted temporary relief. Bevacizumab has shown pain-reducing effects in some case series.

faqs.question: How many schwannomas do patients with schwannomatosis typically have?
faqs.answer: The number varies widely. Some patients have just two or three tumours; others develop dozens over decades. Whole-body MRI typically reveals far more tumours than are clinically apparent, including many asymptomatic intraspinal or paraspinal schwannomas. LZTR1 variants are associated with higher tumour numbers in some series.

faqs.question: Should I get genetic testing if a family member has schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. First-degree relatives of a person with confirmed schwannomatosis should undergo genetic counselling and molecular testing for the family's specific SMARCB1 or LZTR1 variant. If positive, regular MRI surveillance is recommended to catch tumours early. If negative, genetic risk is substantially reduced.

faqs.question: Where are schwannomas located in schwannomatosis?
faqs.answer: Schwannomas can occur on any peripheral, cranial, or spinal nerve. Common locations include spinal nerve roots, peripheral nerves in the limbs and trunk, and occasionally non-vestibular cranial nerves. Spinal tumours, which may be entirely asymptomatic, are present on imaging in the majority of patients.

metaDescription: Schwannomatosis causes multiple schwannomas and chronic nerve pain from SMARCB1 or LZTR1 mutations. Learn diagnosis, pain management, and genetics.
